body:
### Overview

This control ensures that **Amazon EKS Kubernetes Secrets are envelope-encrypted with a customer-managed AWS KMS key**.

### What is checked

The assessment checks whether the EKS cluster encryption configuration includes a KMS provider key ARN for the secrets resource. A cluster with no encryption configuration, or an empty provider key ARN, does not meet the expected state.

### Why it matters

Kubernetes Secrets can contain database passwords, API keys, tokens, and application credentials. EKS encrypts Secrets at rest by default, but using a customer-managed KMS key gives the organization stronger control over key access, auditing, rotation, and administrative separation.

### What good looks like

Each production EKS cluster should use a customer-managed symmetric KMS key for Kubernetes Secrets. Existing Secrets should be rewritten after encryption is enabled so stored data is protected by the new provider.
name: Ensure Kubernetes Secrets are encrypted using Customer Master Keys (CMKs) managed in AWS KMS
remediationDescription:
## Remediation

### AWS

Enable Amazon EKS secret encryption with an AWS KMS customer-managed key. The check verifies that the EKS cluster encryption configuration includes a provider key ARN for Kubernetes Secrets.

1. Create or select a symmetric customer-managed KMS key in the same AWS Region as the EKS cluster.
2. Ensure the EKS service principal and the cluster role can use the key for Secrets encryption.
3. Associate the KMS key with the EKS cluster encryption configuration for the secrets resource.
4. Rewrite existing Kubernetes Secrets so existing data is encrypted by the newly associated provider.
5. Validate that the cluster reports a non-empty encryptionConfig.provider.keyArn.

Associate the key:

~~~bash
aws eks associate-encryption-config \
  --cluster-name {{asset.name}} \
  --encryption-config '[{"resources":["secrets"],"provider":{"keyArn":"<kms-key-arn>"}}]' \
  --region {{asset.region}}
~~~

Rewrite existing Secrets after the encryption provider is active:

~~~bash
kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl replace -f -
~~~

Validate the EKS encryption configuration:

~~~bash
aws eks describe-cluster \
  --name {{asset.name}} \
  --region {{asset.region}} \
  --query 'cluster.encryptionConfig'
~~~

References:
- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/enable-kms.html
- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html

The control is satisfied when the EKS cluster has Secrets encryption configured with a customer-managed AWS KMS key.
description: Amazon EKS clusters that do not encrypt Kubernetes Secrets with an AWS KMS customer-managed key.
